title: AML Agents Briefing — Sunday, 14 June 2026
script:
ALEX: Welcome to AML Agents Briefing for Sunday the 14th of June 2026. This is your audio overview of the most material AML and financial crime developments across the Asia-Pacific region today. Full detail, the obligation register, news sources, and horizon scanning are all at amlagents dot streamlit dot app.

JORDAN: Quiet on the news front today, Alex, but there are some useful threads worth pulling, and the obligations picture deserves attention before we close.

ALEX: Agreed. Let's start with Hong Kong, because a few separate items are converging in a way that matters for compliance leaders there. Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu has signalled further economic reforms in the final year of his current term. On the surface that sounds like a macroeconomic story, but the compliance read-across is real.

JORDAN: Why does that matter for an MLRO or head of financial crime in Hong Kong?

ALEX: Because Hong Kong is under active scrutiny from international standard-setters, including FATF. Reform pledges carry implicit pressure to demonstrate that the city's AML and counter-terrorist financing architecture meets Recommendation 37 and Recommendation 40 standards on international cooperation. Any structural changes to the financial sector, new licensing categories, new product types, expand the regulated perimeter, and compliance functions need to anticipate that before it lands.

JORDAN: So the action item here is forward-looking. If you are the MLRO or deputy MLRO at a bank or licensed corporation in Hong Kong, now is the time to flag to your group head of financial crime that the reform agenda may bring new obligated entities or product lines into scope. Raise that in your next risk committee and make sure your horizon-scanning calendar includes any legislative announcements from the HKSAR government through the rest of 2026.

ALEX: Separately, Hong Kong's national security certification framework has gained renewed attention this week. Subsidiary legislation has clarified procedural requirements for non-national security offences that nonetheless engage national security considerations. That distinction matters for financial crime practitioners because it affects how you handle certain STR disclosures and whether legal privilege arguments apply in the normal way.

JORDAN: This is genuinely nuanced territory. If your firm operates across both Hong Kong and mainland China-facing business lines, you should be talking to legal counsel this week about how the Safeguarding National Security Ordinance interacts with your SAR and STR governance. Make sure your financial crime escalation policy addresses scenarios where national security certification is potentially engaged. That is not a standard clause in most policies, and regulators will ask about it.

ALEX: Shifting our focus to obligations, there are three items practitioners should have on their radar right now. The first is Australia. The AUSTRAC annual compliance report for the calendar year 2024 reporting period was due on the 31st of March 2025 under Part 13 of the AML and CTF Rules. If your Australian reporting entity missed that window, late lodgement attracts daily civil penalties and a misleading attestation carries criminal liability under Section 137.

JORDAN: If you are the MLRO at an Australian reporting entity and you have not confirmed that the CY 2024 annual compliance report was filed through AUSTRAC Online, that is your first call on Monday morning. Pull the lodgement confirmation receipt and file it with your audit evidence pack.

ALEX: The second obligation is Indonesia. Under POJK 12 of 2017, the annual independent review of the AML and CFT programme covering calendar year 2024 was due to the Risk Oversight Committee and the Board of Commissioners by the 30th of June 2025. That deadline has passed, but if your Indonesian entity is still working through remediation from that review, the findings need to be formally escalated before year-end.

JORDAN: For compliance officers at Indonesian banks or multifinance companies, check whether the findings from that review have a formal management response and a remediation tracker. PPATK and the OJK will expect to see evidence of Board-level oversight, not just an internal report sitting in a folder.

ALEX: The third is Hong Kong again. The HKMA AML and CFT Guideline Section 11 self-assessment return for CY 2024 was due by the 30th of September 2025. That required the HKMA-prescribed Excel template and independent validation from Internal Audit.

JORDAN: If that return is filed, keep your Internal Audit sign-off on record. The HKMA has been increasingly focused on the quality of self-assessment evidence during thematic reviews, not just whether the form was submitted.

ALEX: Before we close, two things worth watching this week. First, AUSTRAC has published Tranche 2 industry guidance specifically for legal practitioners, covering customer-engagement triggers, source-of-wealth diligence in conveyancing, and legal professional privilege carve-outs. If your firm has any relationship with law firms as clients or as referral partners, your CDD and EDD procedures need to reflect that guidance now.

JORDAN: And second, AUSTRAC has announced a forty-seven million Australian dollar civil penalty against a major registered digital currency exchange for systemic failures in transaction monitoring, mule-account detection, and SMR timeliness. The enforcement follows a twelve-month investigation. For any registered DCE or virtual asset service provider operating in Australia, the read-across is direct: pull your last six months of SMR timeliness metrics, review your mule-account detection logic, and confirm your transaction monitoring coverage includes both inbound and outbound flows. Do that before your next risk committee, not after a regulator calls.

JORDAN: That is your briefing for the 14th of June. Full detail, the obligation register, and all underlying sources are at amlagents dot streamlit dot app. Well worth bookmarking for your Monday morning.

ALEX: Thanks for listening. We will be back with the next update. Stay sharp.
